> **Goal:** build a working neural network by hand, in pure NumPy, and
> understand every line — from a single neuron to backpropagation. This is the
> **capstone** that ties the entire course together.

## Why this is the capstone

Everything you have learned now collides in one place:

- **vectors & dot products** (Ch. 03) — a neuron's weighted sum is a dot product,
- **matrices** (Ch. 04) — a layer is a matrix multiply; a whole batch flows
  through at once,
- **derivatives & the chain rule** (Ch. 06–07) — backpropagation *is* the chain
  rule, applied layer by layer,
- **gradient descent** (Ch. 12) — the loop that actually trains the weights,
- **the sigmoid & cross-entropy** (Ch. 11) — our output neuron and loss.

A neural network is not a new kind of math. It is the math you already know,
wired together and run on a lot of numbers.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### Notebook 13a — The Perceptron (a single neuron)

A neuron forms a **weighted sum** then applies a nonlinear **activation**:

$$z = w \cdot x + b, \qquad a = \sigma(z).$$

The three classic activations:

$$
\text{sigmoid}(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}\in(0,1), \quad
\tanh(z)\in(-1,1), \quad
\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0,z).
$$

The **perceptron learning rule** trains one neuron on linearly separable data
by nudging weights toward each mistake:

$$w \leftarrow w + \eta\,(y-\hat y)\,x, \qquad b \leftarrow b + \eta\,(y-\hat y).$$

It converges whenever a separating line exists — but **fails on XOR**, because
no single straight line can separate XOR's classes. That failure is what forces
us to add a *hidden layer*.

### Notebook 13b — A 2-layer network & backpropagation

Architecture $2 \to H \to 1$ (input, one hidden layer, output). The
**forward pass** in matrix form:

$$
Z_1 = X W_1 + b_1,\quad A_1 = \sigma(Z_1),\quad
Z_2 = A_1 W_2 + b_2,\quad A_2 = \sigma(Z_2).
$$

The **loss** is binary cross-entropy:

$$
L = -\frac1N\sum_i\big[y_i\log\hat y_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat y_i)\big].
$$

**Backpropagation** = chain rule backwards through the layers. With sigmoid +
cross-entropy the output term collapses beautifully:

$$
\delta_2 = \tfrac1N(A_2 - y),\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_2}=A_1^\top\delta_2,\quad
\delta_1 = (\delta_2 W_2^\top)\odot A_1(1-A_1),\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_1}=X^\top\delta_1.
$$

Then **gradient descent** updates every parameter,
$W \leftarrow W - \eta\,\partial L/\partial W$. Trained on the **two-moons**
dataset, the network learns a **curved** decision boundary — exactly what a
single neuron cannot do.

The explanations here are written in an **Andrew-Ng teaching style**: the
network is framed as a **computation graph** (forward computes and *caches*
values; backprop reuses the cache to push an **error signal** $\delta$ backward,
layer by layer); backprop is presented as the **chain rule applied repeatedly**,
where $\delta$ "measures how much each pre-activation is responsible for the
loss"; there are repeated reminders to **check matrix dimensions** as a debugging
habit; a note (with a tiny demo) on **why weights are initialized randomly**
(symmetry breaking, not zeros); and an emphasis on **vectorization** — processing
the whole batch with matrices instead of a per-example loop.

### Notebook 13c — Activation functions

A focused tour of the nonlinear squash at the heart of every neuron:

- **why nonlinearity is required** — stacking linear layers collapses to a
  single linear map (shown numerically),
- **sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU**, and **softmax** (an *output* activation):
  formula, plot, and **derivative** for each,
- the **vanishing-gradient** problem (sigmoid/tanh derivatives saturate near $0$,
  so gradients shrink through deep stacks) contrasted with ReLU's $0$-or-$1$
  derivative,
- **"dying ReLU"** and how **Leaky ReLU** revives dead units,
- a practical **"which activation to use when"** guide (ReLU hidden default;
  sigmoid/softmax output).

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| A neuron is "just" a dot product | $w\cdot x + b$ then a squash — that's it |
| Why nonlinearity? | stacking *linear* layers stays linear; activations bend space |
| XOR | not linearly separable → needs a hidden layer |
| Backprop | not magic — it is the chain rule, reused right-to-left |
| `sigmoid_deriv` takes the **output** | $\sigma'(z) = a(1-a)$ where $a=\sigma(z)$ |
| Shapes matter | every matrix product must line up; comment the shapes |

## After this chapter

You have built a neural network from first principles and trained it with your
own backpropagation. From here, libraries like PyTorch automate the gradients
(`autograd`) and add more layers — but you now know what is happening underneath.
Congratulations: you've reached the end of the course.

## 📚 Further reading

- Andrew Ng — [Deep Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/deep-learning) (Coursera) — Course 1, "Neural Networks and Deep Learning," teaches exactly this backprop.
- 3Blue1Brown — [Neural Networks](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDNU6R1_67000Dx_ZCJB-3pi) (YouTube) — the clearest visual account of backpropagation.
- [Deep Learning book](https://www.deeplearningbook.org) — Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (free) — chapter 6 on feedforward networks.
- Stanford [CS229 lecture notes](https://cs229.stanford.edu/) — neural networks and backprop with full derivations.
